titlestmt.titleproper: Walter and Naomi Rosenblum Collection of Photography and Photographic History MSS.556
titlestmt.author: Rachel Searcy and Aki Snyder

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2026-02-19 19:45:08 UTC.

change.item: Record updated by Rachel Searcy to reflect January 2019 accretion.
Record updated by Rachel Searcy to reflect 2021 accretion.
Record updated by Rachel Searcy to reflect 2024 accretion.
Digital objects for processed electronic records were created to conform to current practices by Lauren Stark.
Updated by Aki Snyder to incorporate materials from accession 2024.016.

did.unittitle: Walter and Naomi Rosenblum Collection of Photography and Photographic History

physdesc.extent: 90 manuscript boxes, 3 half manuscript boxes, 4 flat boxes, 12 oversized flat boxes, 8 media boxes, 3 custom photo boxes, 2 custom book boxes, 1 CD box, and 7 flat file folders

did.physloc: Some materials are stored offsite and advance notice is required for use. Please request materials at least two business days prior to your research visit to coordinate access.
did.abstract: Walter A. Rosenblum (1919–2006) was an American photographer and member of the Photo League, a cooperative of New York based photographers focused on socially conscious photography. Walter served in World War II and photographed the D-Day landings at Normandy in 1944. He was also the first Allied photographer to enter the liberated Dachau concentration camp. Naomi Rosenblum (1925-2021) was a historian of photography and wrote about individual photographers including Lewis Hine and Paul Strand. She also published several monographs including, A World History of Photography and A History of Women Photographers. The Walter and Naomi Rosenblum Collection of Photography and Photographic History (dated 1860-2018) consists of materials created and collected by the Rosenblums documenting their careers as photographers and historians of photography. Materials in this collection include articles, magazines, and newspaper coverage of Walter's photographic career; catalogs and other ephemera from exhibitions; scrapbooks of Walter's career and his student's photographs; Walter's master photographic prints; and panels from the traveling exhibition They Fight With Cameras: Walter Rosenblum in World War II from D-Day to Dachau. The collection also documents Naomi's career as a historian of photography and includes correspondence, research files, examples of historical photographic processing techniques like daguerreotypes and tintypes, as well as original writings on individual photographers (including Lewis Hine and Paul Strand) and the history of photography in general. Additionally, the collection contains materials documenting the activities of The Photo League in the 1930s-1940s and the Rosenblum's personal items like desk calendars and awards.
did.langmaterial: Materials primarily in English, with some publications in other languages such as Japanese, Russian, Chinese, Italian, French, and German.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Walter and Naomi Rosenblum Collection of Photography and Photographic History (dated 1860-2018) consists of materials created and collected by the Rosenblums documenting their careers as photographers and historians of photography. Materials in this collection include articles, magazines, and newspaper coverage of Walter's photographic career; catalogs and other ephemera from exhibitions; scrapbooks of Walter's career and his student's photographs; Walter's master prints; and panels from the traveling exhibition They Fight With Cameras: Walter Rosenblum in World War II from D-Day to Dachau. The collection also documents Naomi's career as a historian of photography and includes correspondence, research files, examples of historical photographic processing techniques like daguerreotypes and tintypes, as well as original writings on individual photographers (including Lewis Hine and Paul Strand) and the history of photography in general (particularly for her works A World History of Photography and A History of Women Photographers). Additionally, the collection contains materials documenting the activities of The Photo League in the 1930s-1940s. The collection also includes some audiovisual materials containing interviews with the Rosenblums, as well as born-digital items containing essay drafts and photographs.

The Walter and Naomi Rosenblum Collection of Photography and Photographic History also provides some insight about the Rosenblums' personal lives through postcards, personal correspondence, awards, desk calendars, personal photographs, address books, and Naomi's sketch pads. Some materials – mainly film posters – relating to their daughter Nina (a documentarian), can also be found within this collection.
Most of the materials in this collection are written in English, however there are also some items written in Russian, French, Italian, Chinese, and Japanese.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The materials in this collection have been arranged into the following series and sub-series:
Series I: Naomi Rosenblum's Research Materials, Correspondence, Subject Files, and Lectures
Sub-series II.C: Lecture and Publication Materials
Series IV: Walter and Naomi Rosenblum Personal Materials
Within each series and sub-series, some materials have been arranged alphabetically and/or grouped together thematically, when possible.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Box 89 contains legal documents that are restricted. The remaining materials in the collection are open without restrictions.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: This collection is protected by copyright and/or related rights. You are free to use materials in the collection in any way that is permitted by the copyright and related rights legislation that applies to your use. For other uses you need to obtain permission from the rights-holder(s).

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Donated by Naomi Rosenblum in November 2018, January 2019, and December 2021. The accession numbers associated with these gifts are 2018.116, 2019.007, and 2021.040. In February 2024, Nina Rosenblum donated an accretion; the accession number associated with this gift is 2024.016.

phystech.head: Audiovisual Access Policies and Procedures
phystech.p: Audiovisual materials have not been preserved and may not be available to researchers. Materials not yet digitized will need to have access copies made before they can be used. To request an access copy, or if you are unsure if an item has been digitized, please contact Fales Library and Special Collections, special.collections@nyu.edu, 212-998-2596 with the collection name, collection number, and a description of the item(s) requested. A staff member will respond to you with further information.

phystech.head: Born-Digital Access Policies and Procedures
phystech.p: Born-digital materials have not been transferred and may not be available to researchers. Researchers may request access copies. To request that material be transferred, or if you are unsure if material has been transferred, please contact Fales Library and Special Collections, special.collections@nyu.edu, 212-998-2596 with the collection name, collection number, and a description of the item(s) requested. A staff member will respond to you with further information.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Identification of item, date; Walter and Naomi Rosenblum Collection; MSS 556; box number; folder number; Fales Library and Special Collections, New York University.

separatedmaterial.head: Separated Materials
separatedmaterial.p: Camera equipment was separated from the archival collection. Some pieces were transferred to the Tisch School of the Arts Department of Photography and Imaging for teaching purposes while the remaining were returned to the donor.

During processing, a large selection of commercially published books, a Lewis Hine calendar, and various personal correspondence including holiday cards and birth announcements were removed from the collection and either returned to the donor or transfered to Bobst library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: The Walter and Naomi Rosenblum Collection of Photography and Photographic History is comprised of a gift and several accretions from Naomi and Nina Rosenblum. The collection was processed and described by an archivist in the spring of 2022. Materials were placed in new acid-free folders and boxes. Original folder titles were retained when available. Unique ID numbers were assigned to all audiovisual materials. During processing, duplicate materials, commercially published materials, and items of potential sentimental value to the family were removed from the collection.
Photographic materials were stabilized and rehoused by the Barbara Goldsmith Preservation and Conservation Department.

New York University Libraries follow professional standards and best practices when imaging, ingesting, and processing born-digital material in order to maintain the integrity and authenticity of the content. During processing, 5 DVD-R disk were imaged and analyzed using Isobusters Pro and 3 CD-R disks were imaged and analyzed using FTK Imager. Additionally, a single 3.5 in floppy disk was imaged and analyzed using KryoFlux.
File titles were reviewed by the processing archivist and when identified as restricted, these materials were physically separated and stored in a designated restricted box (Box 89).
In April 2024, an accretion of materials was physically rehoused in archival boxes and intellectually added to the collection. The associated accession number is 2024.016.

In February 2025, new digital objects for processed electronic records were created to conform to current practices. One related file in the inventory were reorganized so that individual formats were listed separately.

In February 2026, accretion materials from accession number 2024.016 were arranged and described by an archivist. These materials were added to the inventory in their appropriate series. Materials from this accession are housed in Boxes 112-116.

bioghist.head: Biographical Note
bioghist.p: Walter A. Rosenblum (1919–2006) was an American photographer born in New York City. He was introduced to documentary photography early in his career as a member of the Photo League, which was a cooperative of New York based photographers focused on socially conscious photography. While part of the League, he became friends with notable photographers Paul Strand and Lewis Hine. Walter served as the Photo League's secretary, became editor of the organization's journal (Photo Notes), and in 1941, he became president of the Photo League. During World War II, Walter was a member of the U.S. Army Signal Corp Combat Unit. He photographed the American D-Day landings at Omaha Beach in Normandy in 1944, filmed General Patton's advance across France and Germany, and finally, he was one of the first Allied photographers to enter the liberated Dachau concentration camp. After the war, Walter began teaching photography at Brooklyn College's Art Department. He also taught for several years at Cooper Union and Yale's Summer School of Music and Art. During this time, Walter produced several photographic series focused upon Haiti, Europe, East Harlem, and the South Bronx. Over the course of his career, Walter Rosenblum was the recipient of the Guggenheim Foundation Fellowship, a National Endowment of the Arts Grants, a Simon Weisenthal Award, and other distinctions. He also received an honorary doctoral degree from the University of Maryland in 1986.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: For more information about Walter Rosenblum, see Walter Rosenblum Unitarian Service Committee Papers (TAM.471) and Walter Rosenblum Photographs of Spanish Refugees (PHOTOS.249).

appraisal.head: Appraisal
appraisal.p: One disc, Fundacion Santander Central Hispano y PHE05 presentan la exposición Callie Mayor, was deaccessioned because it could not be imaged.

bioghist.head: Biographical Note
bioghist.p: Naomi Rosenblum (1925-2021) was a historian of photography born in Los Angeles. She wrote about important individual photographers including Lewis Hine, Paul Strand, and Alfred Stieglitz, as well as published several monographs including, A World History of Photography and A History of Women Photographers. After receiving her Bachelor of Arts in fine arts from Brooklyn College in 1948, Naomi completed her Masters in fine arts at CUNY Graduate Center in 1975. She went on to earn her doctorate in art history and the history of photography in 1978 at CUNY Graduate Center. Her doctoral dissertation examined the early work of Paul Strand circa 1920 to 1932. During her career, Naomi curated several museum exhibitions including the traveling exhibit "A History of Women Photographers" in 1997, "Documenting a Myth: The South As Seen By Three Women Photographers" in 1997 at the Douglas F. Cooley Memorial Art Gallery (Reed College), and "America and Lewis Hine, a retrospective of the work of Lewis Hine at the Brooklyn Museum" (1977). The latter exhibit was co-curated with her husband, Walter. She also gave lectures about the history of photography across the United States and abroad.

Walter and Naomi Rosenblum met while he was teaching at Brooklyn College. They were married in 1949 and went on to have two daughters, Nina and Lisa. On May 4, 1998, both Naomi and Walter Rosenblum were awarded the International Center of Photography's Lifetime Achievement Award.

did.unittitle: Series I: Naomi Rosenblum's Research Materials, Correspondence, Subject Files, and Lectures

physdesc.extent: in 49 manuscript boxes, 1 flat box, 4 oversized flat boxes, and 1 flat file folder

did.langmaterial: Materials written in English, French, Spanish, Italian, German, and Chinese

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series I contains Naomi Rosenblum's research notes, correspondence, bibliography lists, lectures, books, articles, newspaper clippings, and photographs relating to the following topics: the history of photography and women photographers, a world history of photography, social photography, Paul Strand, Alfred Stieglitz, Lewis Hine, the Photo League, and examples historical photographic techniques including prints, daguerreotypes, ambrotypes, and tintypes.
Commercially published books used by Naomi Rosenblum as research materials were removed from the collection.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Within this series, correspondence has been arranged alphabetically by correspondent. The remaining materials have been grouped together thematically, when possible.

physdesc.extent: in 27 manuscript boxes, 2 half manuscript box, 2 media boxes, 4 flat boxes, 6 oversized flat boxes, and 7 flat file folder

did.langmaterial: Materials written in English, French, Spanish, Italian, Japanese, and Chinese

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series II contains various materials and subjects related to Walter Rosenblum including photographs, scrapbooks, articles about Walter's career, exhibition materials, research notes, and lecture notes. The series also include audiovisual and born-digital items containing interviews with Walter about his work and career, as well as interviews with colleagues like Paul Strand.
Note: Box 89 contains legal documents that are restricted.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The materials in both the larger series and sub-series have been arranged thematically.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Sub-series II.A contains approximately 116 black and white master photographic prints produced by Walter Rosenblum illustrating his time in France during WWII, his Haiti series, South Bronx series, Pitt Street series, Gaspe series, Europe 1973 series, and East Harlem series.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The arrangement of the photographs in this sub-series is based upon the themes of Walter Rosenblum's photograph projects; photographs belonging to the same series have been grouped together.

physdesc.extent: in 2 manuscript boxes and 4 oversized boxes

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Sub-series II.B contains the traveling exhibit panels from Walter Rosenblum's They Fight With Cameras: Walter Rosenblum in World War II from D-Day to Dachau exhibit, as well as other promotional materials such as invitations, posters, guidebooks, and flyers related to his other exhibitions.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials belonging to the same exhibition(s) were grouped together, when possible.

did.unittitle: They Fight with Cameras: Walter Rosenblum in WWII from D-Day to Dachau Traveling Exhibition Displays

Walter Rosenblum -- They Fight with Cameras Show, Manuela

Documents Relating to Paul Strand & Walter Rosenblum Exhibition
Spanish Refugees Exhibit (In Collaboration with Abraham Lincoln Brigade Archive, ALBA)
Photographs from the Collection of Naomi and Walter Rosenblum Photography: Art and Artifact
"Messages from the Heart" -- Photography by Walter Rosenblum

unittitle.emph: Walter Rosenblum: In Search of Pitt Street

did.unittitle: Sub-series II.C: Lecture and Publication Materials

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Sub-series II.C contains Walter's research and lecture notes, as well as some essays and articles he authored.

Lewis Hine (??) at the International Center for Photography (Walter Rosenblum)

physdesc.extent: in 4 manuscript boxes and 1 oversized box

did.langmaterial: Materials are written in English and Italian

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Photo League, founded in 1936, was a cooperative of New York based photographers focused on socially conscious photography. Notable members included Walter Rosenblum, Paul Strand, and Lewis Hine. Series III contains copies of the organzation's official publication (Photo Notes), papers related to social justice and the Photo League's mission, materials related to Walter's time as members of the League, exhibition materials related to the Photo League, as well as Naomi and Walter's research notes about the organization.

did.unittitle: Press Clippings About the Photo League
Press Clippings about Walter Rosenblum, Paul Strand, Lewis Hine, and the Photo League

unittitle.emph: Paul Strand & The Photo League: A Personal Memoir

did.unittitle: Photo League -- Greenberg Gallery, Jewish Museum
New York Public Library -- Photo League
Dreaming of Tomorrow: Social Documentaries That Moved American Society (in Japanese)
Photo League Works at Queens College
Photography American - 1940s and Notes for Naomi Rosenblum

unittitle.emph: Camera as Weapon: Working Photography between the Wars

did.unittitle: Journal of Homer Folks - Survey Trip for American Red Cross of Civilian Population of European Countries Affected by the War
Photo Notes: Official Publication of the Photo League in New York
Journey to the Cape (Sid Grossman & Millard Lampell)
Photo League, 1936-1951 (College at New Paltz Exhibition)
Article about Photo League - Hadassah Magazine
Magazines with articles about Photo League
"Where Do We Go From Here?" - The Photo League and Its Legacy (1936-2006)
Poster for Ordinary Miracles: The Photo League's New York (film by Nina Rosenblum)
Photo League Exhibition at Jewish Museum and Tolerance Center (Moscow, Russia)
Series IV: Walter and Naomi Rosenblum Personal Materials

physdesc.extent: in 4 manuscript boxes, 1 flat box, and 3 oversized boxes

did.langmaterial: Materials are written in English and Italian

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series IV contains personal items such as the Rosenblums' daily planners, awards, correspondence, address books, postcards, personal photographs, Walter's doctoral degree, materials related to their daughter (Nina), and Naomi's sketchbooks. Some born-digital materials containing photographs are included in this series.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this series have been grouped together thematically or by material type, when possible.
